narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision was driven primarily by your position in Year 3 grades, where you ranked in the 2nd decile—much lower than the typical 6th decile among students who went on to pass the bar—which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting failure. Your LSAT score of 35, while respectable, fell short of the 37.3 average for successful bar takers and provided some counterbalance, nudging the outlook slightly toward passing. Your upper-middle family income aligned with about 45% of those who passed, but it still contributed a bit toward the failure prediction, possibly because the model sees subtler patterns in how backgrounds like yours correlate with bar outcomes in the data. Overall, your Year 3 decile ranking stood out as the biggest factor pulling against you, far outweighing the milder influences from your LSAT and family income. Compared to admitted students, who typically show stronger mid-to-upper decile performance in later years and higher test scores, your profile appeared riskier despite solid early-year results in the 6th decile and full-time study plans. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 reflecting hard work amid other demands. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted tutoring or retaking key courses, aim to retake the LSAT for a score closer to 37 or above, and gather letters highlighting your resilience and preparation strategies. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—consider community programs tailored for aspiring lawyers from diverse backgrounds to build your record. Reapplying after demonstrating improvement could shift the model's view in your favor next time. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
